name: Arizona Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text:
The Arizona Research and Development Tax Credit is authorized under Arizona Revised Statutes (A.R.S.) §§ 43-1168 (corporations) and 43-1074.01 (individuals and pass-through entities). The credit is administered by the Arizona Department of Revenue (ADOR). Arizona's R&D credit conforms closely to federal IRC §41 in defining qualified research expenses (QREs), which include wages paid to employees engaged in qualified research, supplies used in qualified research, and a portion of contract research expenses conducted within Arizona. Only research activities performed within Arizona qualify — expenses attributable to out-of-state research activities do not count.

The headline credit rate is 24% of the excess of Arizona QREs over the base amount for taxpayers with QREs of $2.5 million or less. For QREs exceeding $2.5 million, the rate drops to 15% on the excess above that threshold. This tiered structure means smaller businesses can claim a higher effective rate. The base amount calculation generally mirrors the federal methodology under IRC §41, using a fixed-base percentage applied to average annual gross receipts.

Small businesses — defined as those with fewer than 150 employees worldwide — that have been in operation for fewer than five years and are not publicly traded may qualify for a refundable version of the Arizona R&D credit. Under A.R.S. § 43-1074.01(E) and related corporate provisions, eligible small businesses may apply to ADOR for a refund of up to 75% of any current-year credit that exceeds the taxpayer's tax liability, subject to an annual aggregate cap of $5 million statewide. Once the aggregate cap is reached, no further refunds are issued for that year.

For most taxpayers, the credit is nonrefundable but may be carried forward for up to 15 taxable years. The credit cannot be carried back. Pass-through entities such as S corporations and partnerships compute the credit at the entity level and allocate it to owners, who then claim it on their individual returns.

Arizona requires that research activities be conducted within the state; there is no credit for research performed outside Arizona even if the taxpayer is an Arizona-domiciled business. Additionally, research funded by another party (contract research) must meet the same federal standards under IRC §41 to qualify.

Taxpayers must file Arizona Form 308 (Credit for Increased Research Activities) to compute and claim the credit. The form must be attached to the taxpayer's Arizona corporate income tax return (Form 120) or individual income tax return (Form 140) and filed by the applicable due date, including extensions. For questions or to apply for the refundable portion, contact the Arizona Department of Revenue at azdor.gov.
documentation_required: Completed Arizona Form 308 (Credit for Increased Research Activities)
Federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities) or equivalent workpapers
Payroll records identifying employees and wages allocated to qualified research in Arizona
Time-tracking or project logs documenting qualified research activities and personnel involvement
Contractor agreements and invoices for contract research expenses performed in Arizona
Records of supply purchases used in qualified research
Description of each research project and the technological uncertainty/experimentation involved
Gross receipts records for base period calculation
Business records confirming Arizona-based activity for all claimed QREs
For refundable credit applicants: documentation of employee count and years in operation

name: Arkansas Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text:
The Arkansas Research and Development Tax Credit is authorized under Ark. Code Ann. § 26-51-1102 and administered by the Arkansas Department of Finance and Administration (DFA). For tax years beginning on or after January 1, 2009, the credit is generally equal to 20% of qualified research and development expenditures incurred in Arkansas that exceed a base amount established under the statute. The base amount is computed using a method substantially similar to the federal alternative simplified credit (ASC) methodology under IRC § 41, but applied to Arkansas-sourced research activities only.

To qualify, research expenses must meet the definition of qualified research expenses under IRC § 41, meaning they must be for research conducted within the state of Arkansas, be technological in nature, and meet the four-part test (business component, technological uncertainty, process of experimentation, and qualified purpose). Arkansas conforms broadly to federal § 41 definitions but limits the credit to in-state research activities.

Qualified research expenses include Arkansas wages paid to employees for qualified services, supplies used in Arkansas research, and 65% of contract research expenses paid to unrelated third parties for research performed in Arkansas. Payments to Arkansas colleges and universities for qualified research may receive a higher credit rate — up to 33% for research conducted at Arkansas colleges or universities — making the credit particularly valuable for businesses that partner with state academic institutions.

The credit is non-refundable, meaning it can offset Arkansas income tax liability but will not generate a cash refund. Any unused credit may be carried forward for up to nine taxable years following the year in which the credit was earned. There is no provision for carryback. The credit cannot be transferred or sold to unrelated parties.

Taxpayers must file Arkansas Form AR1100RD (Research and Development Tax Credit) with their Arkansas corporate income tax return (Form AR1100CT) or individual income tax return. Pass-through entities such as S corporations and partnerships may pass the credit through to their owners in proportion to ownership interests.

Small businesses in technology, manufacturing, agriculture, software, and life sciences industries that conduct qualifying research activities in Arkansas are well-positioned to benefit. The DFA may require supporting documentation upon audit. Taxpayers are encouraged to consult the Arkansas Department of Finance and Administration at www.dfa.arkansas.gov or review Ark. Code Ann. § 26-51-1101 et seq. for current rates, forms, and guidance. If uncertain about specific rates or recent legislative changes, see the Arkansas DFA for the most current information.
documentation_required: Completed Arkansas Form AR1100RD
Federal Form 6765 (Research and Experimentation Credit) for reference
Payroll records identifying employees engaged in qualified research in Arkansas
Time-tracking or project records documenting qualified research activities
Supply and materials invoices related to Arkansas research projects
Contracts and payment records for third-party Arkansas research
Records of payments to Arkansas colleges or universities for qualified research
Business component documentation describing research projects and technological uncertainty
Arkansas corporate income tax return (Form AR1100CT) or individual return
Prior-year Arkansas R&D credit carryforward documentation if applicable

name: Colorado Research and Development Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text:
Colorado provides a Research and Development Credit under Colorado Revised Statutes § 39-22-1802 for qualifying research activities conducted within the state. The credit is generally calculated at 3% of qualified research expenses (QREs) that exceed a base amount, closely conforming to the federal research credit framework under Internal Revenue Code § 41. Colorado's credit applies to QREs attributable to research performed in Colorado, meaning that only in-state research activities count toward the credit — a key distinction from the federal credit, which covers domestic research more broadly.

Qualified research expenses for Colorado purposes follow the federal § 41 definitions and include wages paid to employees directly engaged in qualified research, supplies consumed in the conduct of qualified research, and a portion of contract research expenses. The research must meet the four-part test required under federal § 41: (1) there must be a permitted purpose, (2) the activity must be technological in nature, (3) there must be elimination of uncertainty, and (4) there must be a process of experimentation. Basic research payments and university research agreements may also qualify.

The base amount for Colorado is determined in a manner consistent with federal methodology, using a fixed-base percentage and average annual gross receipts for prior years. Startups and companies without sufficient historical data may use the alternative simplified credit (ASC) methodology mirroring federal elections under § 41(c)(5), though taxpayers should consult the Colorado Department of Revenue (CDOR) for confirmation of the applicable methodology.

The Colorado R&D Credit is not refundable; if the credit exceeds the taxpayer's Colorado income tax liability in a given year, the excess may be carried forward for up to five years. There is no carryback provision. The credit is available to C corporations, S corporations, partnerships, and individuals conducting qualifying research in Colorado, with pass-through treatment applicable for flow-through entities.

The credit is claimed on Colorado Form DR 0289 (Research and Development Credit), which must be attached to the taxpayer's Colorado income tax return. The filing deadline aligns with the taxpayer's state income tax return due date, including extensions.

Taxpayers should maintain contemporaneous documentation to substantiate their Colorado R&D credit claims. The Colorado Department of Revenue may audit credit claims, and proper records are essential for defense. For the most current rates, forms, and any legislative updates, see the Colorado Department of Revenue at https://tax.colorado.gov or consult Colorado Revised Statutes § 39-22-1801 et seq.
documentation_required: Completed Colorado Form DR 0289
Federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities)
Payroll records identifying employees engaged in qualified research
Description of qualified research projects and activities performed in Colorado
Time-tracking or allocation records for employee hours on qualified research
Receipts and invoices for research supplies and materials
Contracts and invoices for third-party/contract research expenses
Prior-year gross receipts records for base amount calculation
Documentation of nexus between research activities and Colorado location
Federal and state income tax returns for reference years

name: Connecticut Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate return (Form CT-1120 or CT-1120CU), generally due on the 15th day of the month following the federal due date
eligibility_text: The Connecticut Research and Development Tax Credit is authorized under Conn. Gen. Stat. § 12-217n and administered by the Connecticut Department of Revenue Services (DRS). For income years beginning on or after January 1, 1994, Connecticut-based corporations may claim a credit against the Connecticut corporation business tax for qualified research and experimental (R&E) expenditures conducted within Connecticut. The credit rate is 7.5% of Connecticut qualified research and experimental expenditures in excess of a base amount, conforming broadly to the federal research credit framework under IRC § 41, but the credit applies only to expenditures attributable to research activities conducted within the state of Connecticut. This state-location requirement is a significant distinction from the federal credit: costs must be tied to Connecticut-sited research activities. The base amount computation mirrors the federal methodology, using a fixed-base percentage and average annual gross receipts over the prior four years, subject to the federal alternative simplified credit (ASC) conformity; however, taxpayers should consult the Connecticut DRS for the exact applicable methodology for their tax year, as Connecticut may not conform to all federal election provisions. The credit is non-refundable, meaning it can reduce Connecticut corporation business tax liability to zero but will not generate a cash refund. Unused credits may be carried forward up to 15 income years. Connecticut does not currently offer transferability or sale of this credit to third parties. There is an additional 100% incremental R&D credit available for companies that increase their Connecticut R&D expenditures by more than 10% over the prior year; this incremental credit is also 7.5% on the excess. Small businesses and start-ups should note that the credit requires a positive Connecticut tax liability to be utilized. Entities organized as pass-through entities (S-corporations, partnerships, LLCs) generally do not claim the credit at the entity level; it is a corporation business tax credit. The credit is claimed on Form CT-1120 RDC, which must be attached to the Connecticut corporation business tax return. Taxpayers must maintain contemporaneous records demonstrating that research activities meet the IRC § 41 qualified research definition, including the four-part test (business component, technological in nature, process of experimentation, and elimination of uncertainty), and that those activities occurred in Connecticut. See the Connecticut Department of Revenue Services at portal.ct.gov/DRS for current guidance, instructions, and any legislative updates affecting the credit rate or calculation methodology.
documentation_required: Completed Form CT-1120 RDC attached to the CT corporation business tax return
Federal Form 6765 (Research Credit) for reference and supporting computation
Contemporaneous project records demonstrating qualified research activities under IRC § 41
Payroll records identifying employees and time spent on qualified Connecticut research activities
Records of Connecticut-sited research activities (lab logs, project descriptions, location documentation)
Contracts and invoices for qualified contract research conducted in Connecticut
Gross receipts records for base amount computation (prior four years)
Documentation of supplies and expenditures directly attributable to qualified research

name: Delaware Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or pass-through entity return
eligibility_text:
Delaware offers a Research and Development Tax Credit under Title 30, Section 2053 of the Delaware Code. The credit is available to corporations and pass-through entities conducting qualified research within the state of Delaware. The credit is calculated using a tiered structure based on the federal methodology under IRC §41, adapted for Delaware-based activities only.

For taxpayers with qualified research expenses (QREs) not exceeding $1 million in excess of the base amount, the credit rate is 10% of the incremental QREs (i.e., qualified research expenses above the base period average). For QREs exceeding $1 million above the base amount, the rate is 7% on the amount above that threshold. This tiered structure mirrors the incremental approach of federal §41 but applies only to research activities conducted within Delaware.

Qualified research expenses must meet the four-part test under federal IRC §41: (1) the expenditures must be for research and experimentation activities, (2) the research must be technological in nature, (3) there must be a process of experimentation, and (4) the research must be intended to discover information useful in the development of a new or improved business component. Delaware generally conforms to the federal definition of qualified research expenses, including wages for employees directly engaged in qualified research, supplies used in qualified research, and 65% of contract research expenses paid to third parties.

The base period calculation for Delaware follows the federal §41 fixed-base percentage methodology, comparing current-year Delaware QREs to a historical base period (typically the average QREs from 1984–1988 or a start-up alternative for newer businesses). Taxpayers unable to establish a full base period may use the start-up rules, capping the base period percentage as provided under federal rules.

The Delaware R&D credit is non-refundable, meaning it can reduce Delaware income tax liability to zero but will not generate a cash refund. Unused credits may be carried forward for up to 10 years. There is no carryback provision. The credit cannot be transferred or sold to unrelated third parties.

Eligible taxpayers must file using the appropriate Delaware form: corporations use Form 1100 with Schedule I, while pass-through entities use Form 700. Documentation supporting the credit claim should be maintained for at least four years and may be subject to audit by the Delaware Division of Revenue.

For current rates, forms, and any legislative updates affecting the credit for tax year 2025, taxpayers should consult the Delaware Division of Revenue at https://revenue.delaware.gov or review Title 30, Section 2053 of the Delaware Code. Small businesses and startups are encouraged to consult a qualified tax professional familiar with Delaware-specific R&D credit requirements, as the incremental calculation can be complex.
documentation_required: Federal Form 6765 (or equivalent supporting worksheets)
Delaware QRE schedule detailing wages, supplies, and contract research expenses attributable to Delaware activities
Base period QRE documentation (1984–1988 or start-up period)
Employee time records or affidavits demonstrating qualified research activity
Contracts with third-party research contractors (contract research expense support)
Project-level descriptions identifying technological uncertainty and process of experimentation
Delaware corporate tax return (Form 1100) or pass-through return (Form 700) with Schedule I
Prior-year Delaware tax returns for base period verification

name: Georgia Research Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text: The Georgia Research Tax Credit, authorized under O.C.G.A. § 48-7-40.5, provides a credit against Georgia income tax for qualified research expenses (QREs) conducted within the state. Georgia generally conforms to the federal definition of qualified research expenses as defined under Internal Revenue Code § 41, meaning expenses must relate to activities that constitute qualified research under federal law — including wages for employees directly engaged in research, supplies consumed in the research process, and a portion of contract research expenses paid to third parties performing qualified research on the taxpayer's behalf. The headline credit rate is 10% of the amount by which a taxpayer's current-year Georgia QREs exceed a base amount calculated using a base period. Georgia uses a historical base period methodology consistent with the federal alternative simplified credit framework; taxpayers should consult the Georgia Department of Revenue (DOR) for current base period calculation guidance, as methodology details may affect the effective credit rate. The credit is computed on Form IT-RD and must be filed with the taxpayer's Georgia income tax return (Form 600 for corporations, Form 700 for partnerships, or Form 500 for individuals and pass-through recipients). The Georgia Research Tax Credit is not refundable — any credit generated in a given tax year that exceeds the taxpayer's Georgia income tax liability may not be received as a cash payment from the state. However, unused credits may be carried forward for up to 10 years, providing a meaningful benefit to companies that are not yet profitable or whose Georgia tax liability is temporarily low. Georgia does not currently allow the credit to be transferred or sold to unrelated third parties. To qualify, research activities must be conducted within Georgia; expenses attributable to research performed outside the state are not eligible. There is no statutory cap on the size of the credit for a given taxpayer, making the credit potentially valuable for larger research-intensive companies as well as small and mid-sized businesses. All industries are eligible, including manufacturing, technology, life sciences, agriculture, and professional services, provided the activities meet the federal § 41 qualified research definition. Startups and early-stage companies may benefit particularly from the carryforward provision, as they can accumulate credits against future tax obligations. Taxpayers are strongly encouraged to maintain contemporaneous records documenting the nature and purpose of research activities. For current rates, forms, and guidance, see the Georgia Department of Revenue at https://dor.georgia.gov.
documentation_required: Completed Georgia Form IT-RD
Federal Form 6765 (federal R&D credit computation)
Contemporaneous project descriptions for each qualified research activity
Payroll records identifying employees and time spent on qualified research
Supply and contractor invoices directly related to qualified research
Base period QRE calculations supporting the incremental credit computation
Georgia income tax return (Form 600, Form 700, or Form 500 as applicable)
Documentation of where research activities were physically performed (Georgia nexus)

name: Hawaii High Technology Business Investment Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state income tax return
eligibility_text: Hawaii offers a Research Activities Tax Credit under Hawaii Revised Statutes (HRS) §235-110.91, which conforms broadly to the federal research credit under IRC §41. The credit is available to corporations, partnerships, S corporations, and individuals conducting qualified research activities in Hawaii. The headline credit rate is 20% of qualified research expenses (QREs) paid or incurred during the taxable year for research conducted within the State of Hawaii. Hawaii requires that the research activities occur within the state, meaning out-of-state research expenditures generally do not qualify. The credit is calculated on qualified research expenses as defined by IRC §41, including wages paid to employees directly engaged in qualified research, supplies used in qualified research, and a portion of contract research expenses. Hawaii follows the federal definition of qualified research closely, requiring that research meet the four-part test: it must be for a permitted purpose, be technological in nature, involve a process of experimentation, and eliminate technical uncertainty. There is no alternative simplified credit (ASC) option explicitly codified under Hawaii law; taxpayers should consult the Hawaii Department of Taxation (DOTAX) for guidance on applicable computation methods. The credit is nonrefundable, meaning it can only offset Hawaii income tax liability and cannot generate a cash refund. Unused credits may be carried forward for up to five taxable years. There is no carryback provision. The credit is not transferable or sellable to third parties. Taxpayers must file Schedule HI-7 (or the applicable credit schedule as designated by DOTAX) with their Hawaii state income tax return. For pass-through entities, the credit flows through to the owners in proportion to their ownership interests. Hawaii also separately provides a High Technology Business Investment Tax Credit under HRS §235-110.9, which provides credits for investments in qualified high-technology businesses; however, that credit is distinct from the research activities credit and has had sunset provisions applied in prior years. Taxpayers should verify current availability of both credits with the Hawaii Department of Taxation. The credit applies to taxable years beginning after December 31, 1999, per the original enactment, and has remained available for tax year 2025. Small businesses, technology companies, life sciences firms, and manufacturers conducting experimental research in Hawaii are among the most common claimants. For the most current rates, forms, and instructions, see the Hawaii Department of Taxation at https://tax.hawaii.gov.
documentation_required: Federal Form 6765 (Research Credit) or equivalent workpapers
Payroll records identifying employees performing qualified research and time allocations
Project-level descriptions meeting the four-part test under IRC §41
Supply expense receipts tied to qualified research activities
Contract research agreements and invoices
Hawaii Schedule HI-7 or applicable state credit schedule
General ledger and expense detail for all claimed QREs
Prior-year QRE records for base period computation if applicable

name: Idaho Research Activities Credit
filing_deadline: with state income tax return (typically April 15 for individuals/pass-throughs, or the 15th day of the 4th month after fiscal year end for corporations)
eligibility_text: The Idaho Research Activities Credit is available to taxpayers conducting qualified research activities within Idaho. The credit is governed by Idaho Code § 63-3029B and administered by the Idaho State Tax Commission. Idaho's credit closely conforms to the federal research credit under Internal Revenue Code § 41, meaning that qualifying research expenses for Idaho purposes generally mirror those allowed under the federal definition — including wages paid to employees engaged in qualified research, supplies used in the research process, and a portion of contract research expenses. The headline credit rate is 5% of qualified research expenses (QREs) that exceed a base amount, calculated using the same or a similar base period methodology as the federal credit. In addition, Idaho allows an extra 5% credit on research payments made to Idaho universities and certain non-profit research organizations, making university-partnered research particularly advantageous. To claim the credit, a taxpayer must complete Form 67 (Research Activities Credit) and attach it to their Idaho income tax return. The credit applies to corporations, S corporations, partnerships, and sole proprietors conducting qualified research in Idaho. The research activities must be performed within the state of Idaho; out-of-state research expenses do not qualify for the Idaho credit even if they qualify for the federal § 41 credit. The credit is nonrefundable, meaning it can only offset Idaho income tax liability and will not generate a cash refund if it exceeds the tax owed. Any unused credit may be carried forward for up to 14 years, providing significant flexibility for startups and growing businesses that may have limited tax liability in their early years. There is no provision for carryback of unused credits. Pass-through entities such as S corporations and partnerships calculate the credit at the entity level and pass it through to owners or partners based on their proportionate ownership interests, subject to each owner's individual tax liability. Taxpayers should retain detailed records of qualified research activities and expenses to substantiate any credit claimed. Idaho does not currently allow the credit to be transferred or sold to unrelated third parties. For the most current rates, thresholds, and any legislative changes, taxpayers should consult the Idaho State Tax Commission at tax.idaho.gov or review the most recent version of Form 67 and its instructions.
documentation_required: Completed Idaho Form 67 (Research Activities Credit)
Federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities)
Payroll records identifying employees and time spent on qualified research
Project-level descriptions of qualified research activities and technological uncertainty
Supplier invoices and receipts for research supplies
Contracts and payments for contract research (if applicable)
Documentation of payments to Idaho universities or nonprofit research organizations (if claiming the 5% university credit)
Business financial statements
Prior-year QRE records to support base amount calculation

name: Illinois Research and Development Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text: The Illinois Research and Development Credit is authorized under 35 ILCS 5/201(k) and administered by the Illinois Department of Revenue (IDOR). For tax years beginning on or after January 1, 1990, Illinois allows a credit equal to 6.5% of the taxpayer's qualifying research expenses paid or incurred in Illinois that exceed a base amount, generally conforming to the federal research credit framework under IRC §41. The Illinois credit applies only to qualified research expenses (QREs) attributable to research activities conducted within the state of Illinois, making geographic location of the research activity a key eligibility factor. QREs generally include wages paid to Illinois employees for qualified services, supplies used in Illinois-based research, and contract research expenses for work performed in Illinois. Like the federal credit under IRC §41, Illinois requires a calculation of a base amount using a fixed-base percentage applied to average annual gross receipts. Taxpayers may generally use either the regular credit method or the alternative simplified credit (ASC) methodology, though taxpayers should confirm with IDOR which methods are currently accepted for state purposes. The credit is non-refundable, meaning it can reduce Illinois income tax liability to zero but will not generate a cash refund. Any unused credit may be carried forward for up to 5 years to offset future Illinois income tax liability. There is no carryback provision. The credit is available to corporations, partnerships, S corporations, and individuals who conduct qualified research activities in Illinois. Pass-through entities generally pass the credit to their owners in proportion to their ownership interests. Illinois does not offer a transferability or sale mechanism for this credit. To claim the Illinois R&D Credit, taxpayers must complete Schedule 1299-D (Income Tax Credits) and attach it to their Illinois income tax return (Form IL-1120 for corporations, IL-1040 for individuals, or the applicable pass-through return). Taxpayers should maintain contemporaneous documentation of all qualifying activities and expenditures. The Illinois Department of Revenue may audit R&D credit claims, and taxpayers should be prepared to substantiate the nexus between expenditures and Illinois-based research activities. For the most current rates, rules, and any legislative changes, see the Illinois Department of Revenue at tax.illinois.gov.
documentation_required: Federal Form 6765 (Research Credit) or equivalent workpapers
Illinois Schedule 1299-D
Payroll records identifying employees performing qualified research in Illinois
Time-tracking or project records documenting qualified research activities
Supply and materials invoices tied to Illinois research projects
Third-party contractor agreements and invoices for Illinois-based contract research
Gross receipts records for base amount calculation
Prior-year QRE records for base period calculation
Corporate or individual Illinois income tax return (IL-1120 or IL-1040)

name: Indiana Research Expense Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text: The Indiana Research Expense Credit (REC) is authorized under Indiana Code § 6-3.1-4 and administered by the Indiana Department of Revenue (IDOR). For tax years beginning after December 31, 2021, the credit is generally calculated as 15% of the qualified research expenses (QREs) paid or incurred in Indiana that exceed the base amount, as defined under federal Internal Revenue Code § 41. Indiana conforms to the federal definition of qualified research expenses under IRC § 41, meaning that expenses must relate to a process of experimentation for a purpose intended to be useful in developing a new or improved business component, and the research must be technological in nature. Eligible QREs typically include wages for employees engaged in qualified research activities conducted in Indiana, supplies consumed in the conduct of qualified research in Indiana, and a portion of contract research expenses for research conducted in Indiana. A critical state-specific requirement is that the qualifying research must be conducted within Indiana; expenses attributable to research activities performed outside the state do not qualify for the Indiana credit, even if they would qualify for the federal credit under IRC § 41. The credit is non-refundable, meaning it can only reduce Indiana income tax liability to zero. Any unused credit may be carried forward for up to 10 taxable years. The credit is not transferable. Taxpayers must use the same base period methodology as required under federal IRC § 41, including the fixed-base percentage and average annual gross receipts calculations. Start-up companies that do not have the required prior-year data use the simplified alternative method consistent with federal rules. Small businesses and individuals conducting qualified research may also claim this credit on their Indiana individual income tax returns if the research activity passes through from a partnership, S corporation, or other pass-through entity. Taxpayers must file Schedule IT-20REC with their Indiana corporate income tax return (Form IT-20), or the appropriate schedule with their individual return. For partnerships and S corporations, information is reported on the composite or pass-through returns and allocated to partners or shareholders accordingly. Businesses considering the Indiana Research Expense Credit should carefully document all Indiana-based research activities and associated expenses. The Indiana Department of Revenue may request documentation during an audit, so maintaining contemporaneous records is strongly advised. See the Indiana Department of Revenue (https://www.in.gov/dor/) for current guidance, instructions, and any legislative updates affecting the credit rate or calculation methodology.
documentation_required: Federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities)
Indiana Schedule IT-20REC
Payroll records identifying employees performing qualified research in Indiana
Project-level descriptions of qualified research activities conducted in Indiana
Time-tracking or allocation records for employees engaged in research vs. non-research activities
Receipts and invoices for supplies consumed in Indiana-based research
Contracts and invoices for third-party contract research conducted in Indiana
Base period gross receipts and prior-year QRE records for base amount calculation
Corporate income tax return (Form IT-20) or individual return

name: Iowa Research Activities Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate, individual, or partnership return
eligibility_text:
The Iowa Research Activities Credit is authorized under Iowa Code §422.10 and administered by the Iowa Department of Revenue. Iowa conforms broadly to the federal research credit framework under IRC §41, meaning that qualified research expenses (QREs) must meet the federal definitions: wages for qualified services, supplies used in qualified research, and a portion of contract research expenses. Iowa taxpayers compute the credit using either the regular credit method (Form IA 128) or the alternative simplified credit (ASC) method (Form IA 128S), mirroring the federal election structure.

For the regular credit method, the headline Iowa credit rate is 6.5% of the excess of Iowa QREs over a base amount calculated using the federal base period methodology applied to Iowa-sourced research activities. The alternative simplified credit (IA 128S) allows taxpayers to claim 4.55% (which is 70% of the 6.5% regular rate) of Iowa QREs exceeding 50% of the average Iowa QREs for the prior three tax years, providing a simpler calculation for businesses with limited historical data.

A notable and distinctive feature of the Iowa Research Activities Credit is that it is fully refundable for most taxpayers — meaning if the credit exceeds the taxpayer's Iowa tax liability, the excess is refunded in cash rather than merely carried forward. This makes Iowa's credit particularly valuable for early-stage companies and startups that may not yet have significant Iowa tax liability. Because the credit is refundable, there is no carryforward provision needed for most filers; the full benefit is realized in the year the credit is claimed.

Qualified research activities must be conducted within Iowa. Only wages paid to Iowa employees performing qualified services, Iowa-purchased supplies, and Iowa-based contract research expenses are counted toward Iowa QREs. This geographic nexus requirement means multistate businesses must apportion their research expenditures carefully to identify Iowa-specific QREs.

Small businesses — those meeting the definition under IRC §41(b) — may also be eligible for the Iowa Small Business Credit variant, which provides a slightly different computation. Taxpayers should review the IA 128S instructions carefully to determine which method yields the most favorable result.

Documentation requirements mirror federal §41 standards: contemporaneous records of research activities, employee time logs, payroll records supporting qualified wages, supply purchase receipts, and any contracts with third-party research providers. Iowa may audit research credit claims and request project-level documentation demonstrating that activities meet the four-part test (technological in nature, permitted purpose, elimination of uncertainty, and process of experimentation).

For current rates, any legislative updates, and filing instructions, taxpayers should consult the Iowa Department of Revenue at https://tax.iowa.gov or review the most recent IA 128/IA 128S instruction booklets.
documentation_required: Completed Form IA 128 (regular method) or IA 128S (alternative simplified method)
Federal Form 6765 or equivalent workpapers
Payroll and wage records for employees performing qualified research in Iowa
Time-tracking or project logs documenting qualified research activities
Supply and materials invoices for Iowa-based research
Contracts and invoices for Iowa-based third-party contract research
Project descriptions demonstrating satisfaction of the four-part test under IRC §41
Iowa apportionment workpapers if multistate operations are involved

name: Kansas Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state income tax return
eligibility_text:
The Kansas Research and Development Tax Credit is available to taxpayers conducting qualified research activities in Kansas. The credit is computed under K.S.A. 79-32,182b and generally conforms to the federal research credit framework under IRC §41, requiring that research expenditures meet the four-part test: the activity must constitute qualified research, be technological in nature, have a permitted purpose, and involve a process of experimentation.

The credit rate structure in Kansas is tiered based on the amount of qualified research and development expenditures (QREs) incurred within the state. For taxable years beginning after December 31, 2022, the credit rates are as follows: 10% for QREs up to $1 million; 15% for QREs between $1 million and $5 million; and 16.5% for QREs exceeding $5 million. However, historically the headline rate cited for smaller filers has been approximately 6.5% under older provisions; see the Kansas Department of Revenue (KDOR) for the current applicable rate and tier thresholds for your tax year, as the credit has undergone legislative revisions.

Qualified research expenses must be attributable to research activities conducted within the state of Kansas. This includes wages paid to Kansas-based employees directly engaged in qualified research, supplies used in Kansas-based research, and a portion of contract research expenses paid to entities conducting research in Kansas. The in-state nexus requirement is a key distinction from the federal credit.

The Kansas R&D credit is non-refundable, meaning it can reduce a taxpayer's Kansas income tax liability to zero but any excess credit is not paid out as a cash refund. Unused credits may be carried forward for up to 16 taxable years following the year in which the credit was generated. There is no carry-back provision. The credit applies against Kansas individual income tax, corporate income tax, and privilege tax as applicable.

Eligible taxpayers include corporations, S corporations, partnerships, and individuals engaged in a trade or business in Kansas. Pass-through entities generally pass the credit to their owners proportionally. There is no restriction to a specific industry, though technology, manufacturing, agriculture, and life sciences sectors are among the most common claimants.

To claim the credit, taxpayers must complete Kansas Schedule K-53 and attach it to their Kansas income tax return. Detailed records documenting the nature of the research, personnel involved, expenditures incurred, and the nexus of activities to Kansas must be maintained. Taxpayers should also maintain their federal Form 6765 documentation as a reference.

For authoritative guidance, contact the Kansas Department of Revenue at https://www.ksrevenue.gov or consult K.S.A. 79-32,182b. See the KDOR for current rates if your tax year differs from the examples above.
documentation_required: Kansas Schedule K-53
Federal Form 6765 (for reference and computation)
Payroll records identifying Kansas-based employees performing qualified research
Time-tracking or contemporaneous records documenting research activities
Project descriptions establishing technological nature and process of experimentation
Expense records for supplies used in Kansas research
Contract research agreements and invoices for Kansas-based contract research
General ledger and accounting records supporting QRE totals
Description of qualified research projects and business component nexus

name: Kentucky Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text: The Kentucky Research and Development Tax Credit is authorized under KRS 141.395 and administered by the Kentucky Department of Revenue (DOR). The credit is available to corporations and individuals conducting qualified research activities within the Commonwealth of Kentucky. Kentucky generally conforms to the federal definition of qualified research expenses (QREs) as defined under IRC §41, meaning expenses must be for research undertaken to discover information that is technological in nature, intended for use in developing a new or improved business component, and constitute elements of a process of experimentation. The headline credit rate is 5% of Kentucky qualified research expenses that exceed a base amount, similar in structure to the federal incremental credit methodology. Taxpayers must demonstrate that research activities are conducted within Kentucky and that expenses are allocable to in-state research. The base amount is calculated consistent with federal §41 base period rules, though taxpayers should consult the Kentucky DOR for the precise methodology applicable to their situation and tax year. Kentucky does not make this credit refundable; if the credit exceeds the taxpayer's Kentucky income tax liability for the year, the excess may be carried forward for up to 10 taxable years. There is no provision for transferability or sale of the credit to unrelated third parties. The credit is claimed on Schedule KRDC (Kentucky Research and Development Credit), which must be attached to the taxpayer's Kentucky Form 720 (corporate income tax return) or Form 740 (individual income tax return) as applicable. Pass-through entities such as S corporations and partnerships may pass the credit through to their owners in proportion to ownership interests, and owners then claim the credit on their respective Kentucky returns. Kentucky also participates in the Kentucky Business Investment (KBI) program and other economic development incentives that may layer with the R&D credit for qualifying companies. Small businesses conducting software development, manufacturing process improvement, engineering, life sciences research, and similar technological activities are typical claimants. Taxpayers should maintain contemporaneous documentation including payroll records segregated by project, contractor invoices, project descriptions, experimental design records, and any patent applications. The credit interacts with the federal R&D credit, and taxpayers may not double-count expenses. See the Kentucky Department of Revenue at https://revenue.ky.gov for current guidance, forms, and any legislative updates affecting the credit for 2025 tax years, as rates and rules may be updated by the Kentucky General Assembly.
documentation_required: Federal Form 6765 (or equivalent QRE calculation worksheets)
Kentucky Schedule KRDC
Payroll records allocated by research project
Contractor and vendor invoices for supply and contract research expenses
Project descriptions and technical narratives demonstrating qualified purpose
Time-tracking records for employees engaged in qualified research
Experimental design and testing documentation
General ledger and accounting records supporting QRE allocation
Prior-year QRE records for base period calculation
Any Kentucky DOR correspondence or prior audit findings related to R&D

name: Louisiana Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state income or corporation franchise tax return
eligibility_text:
Louisiana offers a Research and Development Tax Credit under La. R.S. 47:6015, administered by the Louisiana Department of Revenue (LDR). The credit is designed to incentivize qualified research activities conducted within Louisiana and closely conforms to the federal research credit framework under IRC §41, meaning that qualifying expenses generally mirror those recognized at the federal level—including wages for qualified services, supplies, and contract research expenses performed in Louisiana.

Credit Rates: For taxable years beginning on or after January 1, 2022, the credit rates are tiered based on the number of employees in Louisiana: (1) 30% of qualifying research expenses for businesses with 50 or fewer Louisiana employees; (2) 10% for businesses with 51–100 Louisiana employees; and (3) 5% for businesses with more than 100 Louisiana employees. These rates apply to qualifying research expenses that exceed a base amount, generally computed consistent with the federal §41 methodology.

Refundability and Transferability: Louisiana's R&D credit is notably refundable, meaning that if the credit exceeds the taxpayer's liability, the excess may be refunded in cash. This is a distinguishing feature compared to most state R&D credits. Additionally, taxpayers may elect to transfer or sell the credit, providing liquidity options especially valuable for start-ups and pre-revenue companies that have limited tax liability.

Application and Certification: Taxpayers must apply for and receive certification from the Louisiana Department of Economic Development (LED) before claiming the credit. The credit is claimed on Louisiana Form R-6410. The application must be submitted to LED prior to or concurrent with the filing of the return on which the credit is claimed. LED reviews the qualified research activities to ensure they meet Louisiana-specific requirements, including that research activities are conducted in Louisiana.

Carryforward: Any credit amounts not used in the current year may generally be carried forward for up to five years, though refundability makes carryforward less critical for most taxpayers.

Qualified Research: Activities must meet the four-part test consistent with IRC §41: (1) technological in nature; (2) intended to discover information; (3) elimination of uncertainty; and (4) process of experimentation. Funded research and foreign research are excluded, consistent with federal rules. Research activities must be conducted within Louisiana to qualify.

For the most current rates, thresholds, and application procedures, taxpayers should consult the Louisiana Department of Revenue at www.revenue.louisiana.gov and the Louisiana Department of Economic Development.
documentation_required: Completed Louisiana Form R-6410
LED certification letter approving qualified research activities
Federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities) and supporting worksheets
Payroll records identifying wages paid to employees performing qualified research in Louisiana
Documentation of qualified supply expenses and contract research agreements
Project descriptions and technical narratives supporting the four-part §41 test
Base period expense calculations and documentation
General ledger entries supporting all claimed QREs
Corporate or individual income tax return on which the credit is claimed

name: Maine Research Expense Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text: The Maine Research Expense Tax Credit is authorized under 36 M.R.S. § 5219-K (for individual/pass-through filers) and 36 M.R.S. § 5122 and related provisions for corporate filers, administered by Maine Revenue Services (MRS). For tax years beginning on or after January 1, 2014, Maine provides a credit equal to 5% of the excess of qualified research expenses (QREs) incurred in Maine over a base amount, conforming broadly to the federal research credit framework established under IRC § 41. Maine generally conforms to the federal definition of QREs, which includes wages paid to employees engaged in qualified research, supplies used in qualified research, and a portion of contract research expenses, provided those activities occur within the state of Maine. The base amount is computed using a method substantially similar to the federal fixed-base percentage or the alternative simplified credit (ASC) method — taxpayers should consult Maine Revenue Services for the precise base period methodology applicable to their filing situation, as Maine's conformity to federal computational methods may differ from the federal ASC election in some respects. The credit is non-refundable; it may only offset Maine income tax liability and will not generate a cash refund if it exceeds the tax owed in the current year. Unused credit may be carried forward for up to 15 years, providing meaningful benefit for startups and growing companies that may have limited near-term tax liability. The credit is not transferable or assignable. There is no cap on the absolute dollar amount of the credit, but as a practical matter, small and mid-sized businesses typically claim between $2,500 and $75,000 annually depending on their Maine-based research payroll and supply expenditures. The credit applies to corporations, individuals, partnerships, S corporations, and LLCs taxed as pass-throughs, so long as the research activities are conducted in Maine. Taxpayers claiming the credit must attach the relevant Maine schedule to their return. Maine Revenue Services may audit R&D credit claims, and taxpayers should maintain contemporaneous records documenting the nexus of research activities to Maine. See Maine Revenue Services (https://www.maine.gov/revenue/) for current guidance, instructions, and any legislative updates affecting the credit rate or computation for tax year 2025.
documentation_required: Completed Maine research credit schedule attached to Maine return
Federal Form 6765 (Research Credit) or equivalent computation
Payroll records identifying employees and time spent on qualified research in Maine
Project-level descriptions of qualified research activities and the four-part test
Receipts and invoices for research supplies used in Maine
Contracts and payments to third-party contractors for Maine-based research (64% includable)
Base period revenue and QRE records for fixed-base percentage calculation
General ledger and accounting records allocating expenses to qualified research

name: Maryland Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or personal income tax return, typically by April 15 or the extended due date
eligibility_text:
The Maryland Research and Development Tax Credit is authorized under Maryland Code, Tax-General Article § 10-721, and administered by the Maryland Department of Commerce and the Comptroller of Maryland. The credit is available to businesses that incur qualified research and development expenses (QREs) in Maryland, as defined by reference to federal Internal Revenue Code § 41. Maryland's credit conforms to the federal definition of qualified research expenses, meaning expenses must relate to research that meets the four-part test under IRC § 41: technological in nature, intended to be useful in the development of a new or improved business component, experimental in nature, and related to a qualified purpose.

Maryland offers two tiers of the credit. The Basic Research and Development Tax Credit provides a credit equal to 10% of eligible QREs that exceed the Maryland base amount (calculated using the federal base period methodology applied to Maryland-specific research activity). The Growth Research and Development Tax Credit provides an additional credit of up to 10% of QREs that exceed the prior three-year average of Maryland QREs, specifically targeted at businesses demonstrating year-over-year growth in research spending within the state.

Importantly, Maryland caps the total amount of credits awarded statewide each fiscal year. The aggregate annual cap is set by the Maryland General Assembly, and the Maryland Department of Commerce allocates credits on a first-come, first-served basis or proportionally if applications exceed the cap. Small businesses (defined generally as those with fewer than 50 employees) are allocated a separate portion of the annual credit pool, which helps ensure smaller enterprises can access the program. Taxpayers must apply to the Maryland Department of Commerce to receive a credit certificate before claiming the credit on their state tax return.

The credit is not refundable; it may only reduce Maryland tax liability to zero. Unused credits may be carried forward for up to seven years. There is no carryback provision. The credit is claimed on Form RTC filed with the Comptroller of Maryland and must be accompanied by the credit certificate issued by the Department of Commerce.

To be eligible, all research activities must be conducted within the state of Maryland. Out-of-state research expenses do not qualify. Businesses must maintain detailed records of research activities, payroll for research employees located in Maryland, and supply and contract research expenses incurred in Maryland. See the Maryland Department of Commerce (commerce.maryland.gov) and the Comptroller of Maryland for current annual caps, allocation procedures, and any legislative changes to rates or carryforward periods, as these figures may be updated annually.
documentation_required: Federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities)
Maryland Form RTC (Research and Development Tax Credit application)
Credit certificate issued by the Maryland Department of Commerce
Payroll records for Maryland-based research employees
Documentation of qualified research expenses including supplies, contract research, and wages
Project-level descriptions of qualifying research activities
Prior-year Maryland QRE records for base period calculation
Business financial statements
Records demonstrating research meets the IRC § 41 four-part test

name: Massachusetts Research Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate excise return (Form 355 or 355S)
eligibility_text: The Massachusetts Research Credit is authorized under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 63, Section 38M, and is administered by the Massachusetts Department of Revenue (DOR). For tax years beginning on or after January 1, 2015, the credit is equal to 10% of the excess of qualified research expenses (QREs) incurred in Massachusetts over a computed base amount, plus 15% of basic research payments made to Massachusetts-based universities and other qualifying organizations. The base amount is computed using Massachusetts-sourced QREs only, conforming broadly to the methodology under federal Internal Revenue Code §41, but applied exclusively to in-state research activities. Massachusetts generally conforms to the federal definition of qualified research expenses under IRC §41, including wages for qualified services, supplies used in qualified research, and contract research expenses, but only activities conducted within the Commonwealth qualify for the credit. Taxpayers may elect either the regular credit method or an alternative simplified credit (ASC) method, mirroring the federal election structure. Under the ASC method available at the Massachusetts level, the credit rate is effectively 10% of QREs above 50% of the average annual QREs for the three preceding years conducted in Massachusetts. The Massachusetts Research Credit is not refundable; it may only offset Massachusetts corporate excise tax liability. Unused credit may be carried forward for up to 15 years. There is no carryback provision. The credit is not transferable or sellable. Importantly, the credit is subject to an annual cap: the total credit available in a single year is limited to 100% of the first $25,000 of excise tax liability plus 75% of excise tax liability exceeding $25,000. This effectively means that corporations with larger tax liabilities can use more of the credit in a given year, while excess credit is carried forward. Taxpayers must file Schedule RC with their Massachusetts corporate excise return (Form 355 for C corporations or Form 355S for S corporations). Combined reporting groups must aggregate their Massachusetts QREs and apply the credit at the group level. Pass-through entities such as partnerships and S corporations may pass the credit through to members or shareholders, who then claim it on their individual or corporate returns. The credit is available to businesses of any size and industry, provided the research activities are conducted within Massachusetts. For the most current rates, rules, and instructions, taxpayers should consult the Massachusetts Department of Revenue at mass.gov or contact the DOR directly.
documentation_required: Completed Massachusetts Schedule RC
Federal Form 6765 (for reference and QRE substantiation)
Payroll records documenting wages paid to employees performing qualified research in Massachusetts
Contemporaneous project descriptions identifying qualified research activities
Timekeeping records allocating employee time to qualified research vs. non-research activities
Supply receipts and invoices for materials used in qualified research
Contracts and invoices for contract research expenses (65% includable)
Basic research payment agreements with Massachusetts universities or qualifying organizations
Documentation of base period QREs for prior years (three-year average for ASC method)
Corporate excise tax return (Form 355 or 355S) for the claim year

name: Minnesota Research Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text:
The Minnesota Research Credit is authorized under Minnesota Statutes section 290.068 and administered by the Minnesota Department of Revenue. For tax year 2025, the credit is generally equal to 10% of the first $2 million in qualified research expenses (QREs) that exceed the base amount, and 2.5% of QREs exceeding $2 million above the base amount. The credit applies to research conducted within Minnesota, meaning qualifying activities must occur in the state.

Minnesota generally conforms to the federal research credit framework under Internal Revenue Code section 41, adopting the same definitions of qualified research expenses, including wages paid to employees engaged in qualified research, supplies used in the research process, and a portion of contract research expenses. The research must be undertaken for the purpose of discovering technological information intended for use in developing a new or improved business component, and must meet the process of experimentation standard. Basic research payments may also qualify.

The base amount calculation follows federal §41 methodology, using a fixed-base percentage applied to average annual gross receipts for a four-year base period (or the simplified credit method alternative). Taxpayers should carefully determine whether to use the regular credit method or the alternative simplified credit method, as the calculation significantly affects the credit amount. See the Minnesota Department of Revenue for current guidance on available computation methods.

The Minnesota Research Credit is nonrefundable—it cannot reduce the taxpayer's liability below zero. However, any unused credit may be carried forward for up to 15 years. There is no carryback provision. The credit is available to C corporations, S corporations, partnerships, and individuals conducting qualified research in Minnesota, with pass-through treatment available for owners of flow-through entities.

Eligible businesses must complete Schedule RD, which mirrors the federal Form 6765 but is adjusted for Minnesota-only activities. Only expenses attributable to research conducted within Minnesota's borders are eligible; out-of-state research activities do not qualify for the state credit even if they qualify for the federal credit. Businesses claiming this credit should maintain detailed records distinguishing in-state from out-of-state research activities.

The credit cannot be transferred or sold. There is no separate small-business provision or payroll tax offset (unlike the federal credit for qualified small businesses). Taxpayers should consult the Minnesota Department of Revenue at www.revenue.state.mn.us or review Minnesota Statutes §290.068 for the most current rates and any legislative changes affecting tax year 2025.
documentation_required: Completed Minnesota Schedule RD
Federal Form 6765 (for reference and cross-check)
Detailed payroll records identifying employees engaged in qualified research in Minnesota
Time-tracking records or contemporaneous employee affidavits allocating time to qualified research activities
Supplier invoices and receipts for research supplies and materials
Contracts and invoices for third-party contract research, with documentation of Minnesota-based activities
Project descriptions documenting the technological uncertainty, process of experimentation, and business component goals
Gross receipts records for base period calculation (4 years)
Financial statements and general ledger excerpts supporting QRE amounts

name: Nebraska Research and Development Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text:
Nebraska offers a Research and Development Credit under Neb. Rev. Stat. § 77-2734.04, providing a credit equal to 15% of the excess of qualified research expenses (QREs) paid or incurred in Nebraska during the tax year over a base amount, generally conforming to the federal research credit methodology under IRC § 41. Taxpayers must incur qualified research expenses within Nebraska to be eligible; out-of-state research activities do not qualify for the state credit. The credit is computed using the federal definition of qualified research expenses, including wages paid to employees engaged in qualified research, supplies used in qualified research, and a portion of contract research expenses, provided such activities occur within Nebraska.

The base amount calculation largely mirrors the federal approach under IRC § 41, using a fixed-base percentage applied to the taxpayer's average annual gross receipts for the four preceding tax years, with a minimum base amount equal to 50% of the current year's Nebraska QREs. Taxpayers who do not have sufficient history may use the start-up rules analogous to the federal alternative simplified credit approach; see the Nebraska Department of Revenue for guidance on the applicable methodology.

The Nebraska R&D credit is non-refundable; it may not exceed the taxpayer's state income tax liability for the year. Any unused credit may be carried forward for up to 15 years, allowing businesses undertaking long-term research projects to benefit over time even if current-year tax liability is limited. There is no carryback provision.

The credit is available to corporations, partnerships, limited liability companies, and individuals (including sole proprietors and pass-through entity owners) that incur qualifying Nebraska research expenses. The credit is claimed on Nebraska Form 3800N (Nebraska Nonrefundable Credits) and must be filed with the taxpayer's Nebraska income tax return. Detailed documentation supporting the federal §41 QRE computation—limited to Nebraska activities—must be maintained.

Nebraska does not currently offer a transferability or refundability option for the standard R&D credit under this statute, though certain incentive programs (such as those under the Nebraska Advantage Act or ImagiNE Nebraska Act) may provide alternative or additional research-related incentives for qualifying large projects. Taxpayers engaged in significant research and development investment should consult those programs separately.

For current rates, forms, instructions, and any legislative updates, taxpayers should consult the Nebraska Department of Revenue at https://revenue.nebraska.gov/ or contact the Department directly. Rates and rules are subject to change by the Nebraska Legislature.
documentation_required: Completed federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities) or equivalent computation limited to Nebraska activities
Nebraska Form 3800N (Nonrefundable Credits)
Payroll records identifying employees engaged in qualified research in Nebraska
Time-tracking or project records documenting the nature and scope of qualifying research activities
Supplier invoices and records for research supplies used in Nebraska
Contract research agreements and payment records
Description of qualified research activities and the technological uncertainty or experimentation undertaken
Prior-year Nebraska QRE records for base period calculation
Gross receipts records for base period years

name: New Hampshire Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state business profits tax return
eligibility_text:
New Hampshire offers a Research and Development Tax Credit under RSA 77-A:5, XIII, administered by the New Hampshire Department of Revenue Administration (NHDRA). The credit is equal to 10% of qualifying research and development expenditures paid or incurred during the tax year, subject to an annual statewide cap of $7 million in total credits awarded. Because the statewide cap can be reached quickly, the NHDRA allocates credits on a pro-rata basis among all qualifying claimants if total claims exceed the cap in a given year. This means individual taxpayers may receive less than the full 10% of their QREs depending on the level of total statewide claims.

Qualifying research and development expenses generally conform to the federal definition under IRC §41, meaning expenses must be paid or incurred in the conduct of qualifying research — including wages for employees engaged in qualified research, supplies used in qualified research, and contract research expenses — subject to New Hampshire-specific requirements. Critically, the qualifying research activities must be conducted within New Hampshire; out-of-state R&D expenditures do not qualify for the state credit.

The credit is applied against the Business Profits Tax (BPT) liability. It is not refundable, meaning taxpayers cannot receive a cash payment if the credit exceeds their BPT liability. However, unused credits may be carried forward for up to five years following the tax year in which the credit was generated. There is no carryback provision.

To claim the credit, taxpayers must file Form DP-165 (Research and Development Tax Credit Application) with the NHDRA. There is an annual filing deadline for the application; taxpayers are advised to check with the NHDRA for current submission deadlines, as the department typically requires the application to be submitted by a specified date after the close of the tax year to participate in the annual allocation process. The credit is also available to pass-through entities; members, partners, or shareholders may claim their proportionate share of the credit against their own BPT or Business Enterprise Tax (BET) liabilities.

New Hampshire does not have a broad personal income tax, so the credit primarily benefits C-corporations and pass-through entities subject to BPT. Businesses should carefully document all R&D activities conducted within New Hampshire, maintain payroll records identifying employees performing qualified research, and retain contemporaneous project records linking expenditures to qualifying activities. For the most current cap amounts, allocation procedures, and application deadlines, see the New Hampshire Department of Revenue Administration at https://www.revenue.nh.gov.
documentation_required: Completed Form DP-165 (R&D Tax Credit Application)
Federal Form 6765 or equivalent federal R&D calculation workpapers
Payroll records identifying employees performing qualified research in New Hampshire
Time-tracking records allocating employee hours to qualified versus non-qualified activities
Supplier and vendor invoices for qualifying research supplies purchased
Contract research agreements and payments to third-party researchers
Project descriptions and technical documentation supporting qualified research activities
Prior-year R&D credit carryforward schedules if applicable
Business Profits Tax return (Form BPT-1) to which the credit is applied

name: New Jersey Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate business tax return (CBT-100 or CBT-100S)
eligibility_text: The New Jersey Research and Development Tax Credit is authorized under N.J.S.A. 54:10A-5.24 and administered by the New Jersey Division of Taxation. The credit is available to corporations subject to the New Jersey Corporation Business Tax (CBT) that incur qualified research expenses (QREs) within New Jersey. The credit rate is 10% of the excess of QREs paid or incurred during the tax year over a base amount, closely conforming to the incremental methodology of the federal credit under IRC §41. In addition, New Jersey provides an alternative simplified credit (ASC) methodology, generally mirroring the federal ASC option, at a rate of 6% of the excess of current-year QREs over 50% of the average QREs for the three preceding tax years. Only research activities conducted within New Jersey qualify; expenses attributable to research performed outside the state are not eligible. Qualifying expenses generally include in-state wages for employees engaged in qualified research, supplies used in qualified research conducted in New Jersey, and 65% of contract research payments made to third parties for research performed in New Jersey, consistent with the federal §41 definitions. The credit is not refundable — if the credit exceeds the taxpayer's CBT liability in a given year, the excess cannot be refunded as cash. Unused credit may be carried forward for up to seven years. There is no carryback provision. The credit cannot be transferred or sold to unrelated third parties. Taxpayers must file Form 306 (Research and Development Tax Credit) with their New Jersey CBT return (Form CBT-100 for C corporations or CBT-100S for S corporations). Taxpayers must maintain contemporaneous documentation supporting the qualified nature of the research activities and the New Jersey nexus of the expenses. The credit applies to tax years beginning on or after January 1, 1994, and remains active for tax year 2025. For the most current rates, calculation methods, and filing requirements, taxpayers should consult the New Jersey Division of Taxation at https://www.njtaxation.org or review the instructions accompanying Form 306. Small and mid-size businesses engaged in technology, life sciences, manufacturing, engineering, and software development are among the most common claimants, though the credit is available to any qualifying corporation across all industries. See the New Jersey Division of Taxation for current guidance on any legislative changes affecting the credit for tax years beginning in 2025.
documentation_required: Completed New Jersey Form 306
Federal Form 6765 (Research Credit) for reference
Payroll records identifying employees and time spent on qualified research activities in New Jersey
Project descriptions and technical narratives supporting the qualified research designation
Supplier and vendor invoices for research supplies purchased and used in New Jersey
Contracts and invoices for third-party contract research performed in New Jersey
General ledger and accounting records segregating NJ-located R&D expenses
Prior-year QRE records for base period calculation
Documentation of nexus of research activities to New Jersey locations

name: New Mexico Technology Jobs and Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate income tax or personal income tax return
eligibility_text: New Mexico offers the Technology Jobs and Research and Development Tax Credit under NMSA 1978, Section 7-9F-1 et seq., administered by the New Mexico Taxation and Revenue Department (TRD). The credit is available to businesses that pay qualified research and development expenses within New Mexico, broadly conforming to the federal definition of qualified research expenses under IRC §41. The headline credit rate is 5% of qualified research expenses incurred in New Mexico. An additional 10% supplemental credit rate may apply if the taxpayer has at least $75,000 in qualified research and development expenses and the taxpayer's employees performing the research are located in a rural zone or if the taxpayer's payroll increases by at least 5% above the prior year's level, subject to TRD verification. The credit was originally enacted as a gross receipts tax credit for certain technology jobs; the R&D component allows a credit against either the state corporate income tax or personal income tax, depending on the taxpayer's entity type. Qualifying expenses generally mirror the federal IRC §41 framework and include wages for employees engaged in qualified research, amounts paid for supplies used in research, and contract research expenses for work performed within New Mexico. Unlike the federal credit, New Mexico requires that the qualifying research activities be physically conducted within the state. The credit is not refundable; if the credit exceeds the taxpayer's tax liability in the year it is claimed, the excess may be carried forward for up to three years. Notably, the Technology Jobs Credit (the gross receipts/compensating tax component) and the R&D income tax credit are related but distinct credits under New Mexico law. Taxpayers seeking the income tax credit must complete Form RPD-41385 and submit it with their New Mexico income tax return. The New Mexico TRD may require supporting documentation substantiating the nature and amount of qualified research expenses. Businesses should maintain detailed payroll records showing time spent on qualifying research, contractor agreements, and expense records. The credit is available to taxpayers of all sizes and industries, though in practice it is most commonly claimed by technology, aerospace, manufacturing, and life sciences companies conducting significant research and development activities in New Mexico. Taxpayers should consult the New Mexico Taxation and Revenue Department at its official website or contact TRD directly for the most current rates, forms, and instructions, as the rules have been amended periodically. See the New Mexico TRD for current rates and any updates to the program.
documentation_required: Completed Form RPD-41385
Federal Form 6765 (for reference to qualified research expense computation)
Payroll records identifying employees performing qualified research in New Mexico
Time-tracking records or project logs for research activities
Expense records for supplies and contractor costs related to qualified research
Contractor or vendor agreements for contract research performed in New Mexico
Description of qualified research activities and technological uncertainty
Prior-year payroll records if claiming the supplemental credit based on payroll increase

name: New York Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: Filed with the New York State corporate franchise tax return (Form CT-3 or CT-3-A) by the original or extended due date
eligibility_text:
New York State offers a Research and Development Tax Credit under Tax Law Section 210-B.45 (for Article 9-A corporate franchise taxpayers) and related provisions. The credit has two primary components. The first is the Basic Research and Development Credit, equal to 9% of qualified research expenses (QREs) paid or incurred for research conducted in New York State. The second component is a credit for Basic Research Payments made to qualified organizations under IRC §41(e), also at 9%. New York substantially conforms to the federal definition of qualified research under IRC §41, meaning expenses must satisfy the four-part test: (1) they must be technological in nature, (2) relate to a new or improved business component, (3) involve a process of experimentation, and (4) be intended to discover information to eliminate uncertainty. However, a critical distinction is that only research activities physically conducted within New York State qualify — expenses for out-of-state research do not count toward the New York credit, even if they qualify for the federal credit under IRC §41.

New York's R&D credit is refundable for qualified emerging technology companies (QETCs) and certain small businesses, making it particularly valuable for startups and early-stage companies that may not have significant tax liability. For non-QETC filers, any credit that exceeds the taxpayer's tax liability may be carried forward up to 15 years. There is no carryback provision.

For QETCs, New York also provides an enhanced credit: the Qualified Emerging Technology Company (QETC) Credit under Tax Law §210-B.46, which provides a credit of up to 10% of the increase in research expenditures over a base amount, and 10% of basic research payments — with refundability available for qualifying companies. A company qualifies as a QETC if it has fewer than 100 employees, provides products or services in a qualified emerging technology field (such as biotechnology, information technology, or advanced materials), and has a ratio of research expenditures to net sales of at least 6%, or has QREs of at least $100,000.

The credit is claimed on New York State Form CT-636 (R&D Tax Credit), which must be attached to the taxpayer's franchise tax return. The filing deadline coincides with the corporate return deadline, including extensions. Taxpayers should maintain contemporaneous documentation of all qualifying activities. The New York State Department of Taxation and Finance administers this credit. See https://www.tax.ny.gov for the most current rates, guidance, and any legislative updates prior to filing.
