documentation_required: Completed New York Form CT-636
Federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities)
Contemporaneous project records identifying qualified research activities conducted in New York State
Payroll records showing time employees spent on qualified research
Records of supplies, contractor costs, and lease/rental costs attributable to New York-based research
Documentation supporting any basic research payments to qualified organizations
Records demonstrating QETC status (if applicable), including employee count, revenue figures, and ratio of research expenditures to net sales
New York corporate franchise tax return (Form CT-3 or CT-3-A)

name: North Dakota Research and Experimental Costs Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text:
North Dakota offers a Research and Experimental Costs Credit under N.D. Cent. Code § 57-38-30.5, administered by the North Dakota Office of State Tax Commissioner. The credit is available to corporations and individuals who incur qualified research and experimental expenditures within North Dakota. The headline credit rate is 25% of the excess of current-year qualified research expenses (QREs) over a base amount, broadly conforming to the federal Internal Revenue Code § 41 definitions for qualified research expenses, qualified research, and the base period methodology. Taxpayers must conduct the qualifying research activities within North Dakota to claim the state credit—expenses attributable to research performed outside North Dakota are not eligible.

The credit is calculated on the incremental excess of QREs above the base amount, which is computed using the taxpayer's average annual gross receipts and a fixed-base percentage, consistent with the federal § 41 approach. Startups and other taxpayers who lack sufficient base-period history may use the alternative simplified credit (ASC) method, mirroring available federal elections, though taxpayers should confirm with the North Dakota Office of State Tax Commissioner whether specific federal ASC elections translate directly under state law.

The credit is nonrefundable—it may offset North Dakota income tax liability but any excess credit is not refunded in cash. Unused credit amounts may be carried forward up to 15 years, allowing taxpayers sufficient time to absorb the credit against future North Dakota income tax. There is no carryback provision.

The credit applies to all industries engaged in qualified research, including manufacturing, technology, agriculture-related research, energy, life sciences, and professional services, as long as the research meets the four-part test under federal § 41 (i.e., technological in nature, intended to discover information, involves a process of experimentation, and relates to a new or improved business component).

Taxpayers claim the credit using Form RZ (Research Credit) filed with their North Dakota corporate income tax return (Form 40) or individual income tax return (Form ND-1) for pass-through entities. The return and credit form are generally due on the same timeline as the entity's North Dakota income tax return, including extensions.

For partnerships, S corporations, and other pass-through entities, the credit is computed at the entity level and passed through to the partners, members, or shareholders in proportion to their ownership interests.

Small businesses with modest qualifying research activity might expect credits in the range of $5,000 to $75,000, while larger research-intensive companies may claim substantially more. Taxpayers are encouraged to consult the North Dakota Office of State Tax Commissioner or visit their official website for current guidance, form updates, and any legislative changes affecting the credit rate or carryforward period. See the North Dakota Office of State Tax Commissioner for current rates and instructions.
documentation_required: Federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities)
North Dakota Form RZ (Research Credit)
Project-level description of qualified research activities conducted in North Dakota
Payroll records identifying wages paid to employees engaged in qualified research
Contractor invoices and agreements for third-party research conducted in North Dakota
Supply purchase records directly used in qualified research
Contemporaneous time-tracking records or percentage-of-time estimates for research employees
Gross receipts records for base period calculation
Prior-year North Dakota tax returns showing carryforward amounts
Documentation of nexus and North Dakota location of research activities

name: Ohio Research and Development Investment Tax Credit
form: Ohio Schedule IT RC (or Form IT-1140/IT-4708 with attached credit schedule)
filing_deadline: with state corporate or pass-through entity return
eligibility_text: Ohio's Research and Development Investment Tax Credit is authorized under Ohio Revised Code (ORC) § 5726.50 (commercial activity tax context) and ORC § 5747.33 / § 5733.352 for income and franchise tax purposes. As of 2025, the credit equals 7% of Ohio qualified research expenses (QREs) that exceed a base amount calculated consistent with the methodology used under federal Internal Revenue Code § 41. Ohio substantially conforms to the federal § 41 definition of qualified research expenses, meaning expenses must relate to research undertaken for discovering information that is technological in nature, intended to be useful in developing a new or improved business component, and conducted through a process of experimentation. Activities must be conducted in Ohio or attributable to Ohio-based operations to qualify. The credit is nonrefundable, meaning it can only offset Ohio tax liability and will not generate a cash refund. Unused credit may be carried forward for up to seven years. The credit is not transferable. Taxpayers subject to the Ohio Commercial Activity Tax (CAT), corporate franchise tax, or individual income tax (including pass-through entity owners) may be eligible depending on their entity type and tax profile. Ohio updated its tax structure significantly with the phase-out of the corporate franchise tax, so taxpayers should confirm which Ohio tax return the credit applies to for their particular situation—see the Ohio Department of Taxation for current guidance. Pass-through entities may claim the credit on Form IT-1140 or IT-4708, allocating the credit to their owners. The base amount calculation follows the federal incremental method: taxpayers must establish a base period average of QREs and only expenses exceeding that threshold generate the 7% credit. Startups or companies without adequate base-period history should refer to the alternative simplified credit method available under § 41 and Ohio's conforming provisions. Ohio does not offer a separate enhanced rate for small businesses or startups, but the standard 7% rate applies equally to qualifying filers of all sizes. To claim the credit, taxpayers complete the applicable credit schedule attached to their Ohio return and must maintain contemporaneous documentation supporting each element of the qualified research. For questions, contact the Ohio Department of Taxation at tax.ohio.gov.
documentation_required: Federal Form 6765 (Research Credit) or equivalent workpapers
Ohio credit schedule / Schedule IT RC
Payroll records for employees engaged in qualified research in Ohio
Contracts and invoices for contract research expenses
Project-level documentation describing the nature of research activities and technological uncertainty
Time-tracking records allocating employee hours to qualifying vs. non-qualifying activities
Records of supplies and equipment used in qualified research
Base period QRE calculations supporting the incremental credit computation
Ohio apportionment schedules if applicable

name: Pennsylvania Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate net income tax return or personal income tax return, by the application deadline (typically September 15 of the year following the tax year)
eligibility_text: The Pennsylvania Research and Development Tax Credit (PA R&D Credit) is authorized under the Tax Reform Code of 1971, Article XVII-D, as administered by the Pennsylvania Department of Revenue (PA DOR) and the Pennsylvania Department of Community and Economic Development (DCED). For tax years beginning after December 31, 2000, qualifying businesses may claim a credit equal to 10% of the increase in qualified research and development expenses (QREs) conducted within Pennsylvania over a base amount, consistent with the incremental methodology. A small business rate of 20% applies to qualifying small businesses, generally defined as businesses with fewer than 25 full-time employees and gross revenues of $5 million or less during the taxable year. Pennsylvania conforms broadly to the federal definition of qualified research expenses under IRC §41, requiring that research be undertaken for discovering information that is technological in nature, intended for use in developing a new or improved business component, and conducted within a process of experimentation. The research must be performed within the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania to qualify for the state credit. The PA R&D Credit is not refundable; however, unused credits may be carried forward for up to 15 taxable years. The credit cannot be carried back. Pennsylvania operates a competitive, application-based program with an annual statewide cap on total credits awarded, which has historically been set at $55 million per fiscal year. Businesses must apply to the DCED and PA DOR by the statutory deadline, typically September 15 following the close of the taxable year. Credits are awarded on a pro-rata basis if applications exceed the annual cap, so the actual credit received may be less than the computed amount. To apply, taxpayers file Form RCT-128C (Research and Development Tax Credit Application) with the DCED and PA DOR. Both C corporations subject to the Pennsylvania Corporate Net Income Tax and pass-through entities whose owners are subject to the Pennsylvania Personal Income Tax may be eligible. The credit is applied against the taxpayer's Pennsylvania tax liability. Taxpayers are encouraged to review the PA DCED and PA DOR guidance annually, as program caps and procedural requirements may change. See the Pennsylvania Department of Revenue and Pennsylvania DCED websites for the most current rates, caps, and application instructions.
documentation_required: Completed Form RCT-128C (PA R&D Tax Credit Application)
Federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities)
Detailed records of qualified research expenses conducted in Pennsylvania
Payroll records supporting Pennsylvania-based research wages
Contracts and invoices for Pennsylvania-based contract research
Documentation supporting the base period calculation and incremental QRE methodology
Business financial statements and revenue documentation
Records distinguishing Pennsylvania-located research from out-of-state activities
Documentation of small business eligibility if claiming the 20% rate (employee count and gross revenue records)

name: Rhode Island Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or personal income tax return
eligibility_text:
Rhode Island offers a Research and Development Tax Credit under Rhode Island General Laws § 44-32-1 et seq. The credit is structured to conform broadly to the federal research credit framework under IRC § 41, with qualifying research expenses (QREs) defined in a manner consistent with federal definitions, including wages for qualified services, supplies, and contract research expenses for research conducted within Rhode Island.

The credit is computed at 22.5% of the excess of a taxpayer's Rhode Island QREs for the taxable year over a base amount. The base amount is calculated using a fixed-base percentage applied to average annual gross receipts for the prior four taxable years, mirroring the federal regular research credit methodology. Alternatively, taxpayers may be eligible to use a simplified credit method; see the Rhode Island Division of Taxation for current guidance on available computation methods.

For certain small businesses and start-up companies that have not had sufficient prior-year QRE history to establish a base period, alternative computation rules may apply. The research activities must be conducted within Rhode Island for the expenses to qualify for the state credit.

The credit is not refundable — it may only be used to offset Rhode Island tax liability. If the credit exceeds the taxpayer's tax liability in a given year, the unused portion may be carried forward for up to seven taxable years. There is no carryback provision.

Eligible taxpayers include C corporations, S corporations, partnerships, and individuals conducting qualified research in Rhode Island. The credit is claimed on Form RI-7695, which must be completed and attached to the Rhode Island corporate income tax return (Form RI-1120C), the business corporation tax return, or the appropriate personal income tax return depending on entity type.

The Rhode Island Division of Taxation administers this credit. Taxpayers should consult the Division of Taxation's website and current instructions for Form RI-7695 for the most up-to-date computation rules, rates, and any legislative changes. Rhode Island may also offer an additional credit increment for research conducted in partnership with Rhode Island colleges or universities; see the Division of Taxation for current details on this supplemental credit.

For questions about eligibility, computation, or documentation requirements, taxpayers are encouraged to contact the Rhode Island Division of Taxation directly or consult the most recent version of Publication for Business Tax Credits issued by the Division.
documentation_required: Completed Form RI-7695
Federal Form 6765 (Research Credit) for reference
Payroll records identifying employees performing qualified research in Rhode Island
Time-tracking or project documentation for research personnel
Vendor and contractor invoices for qualified contract research
Supply expense records tied to qualified research activities
Documentation of base period gross receipts and prior-year QREs
Project-level technical documentation supporting the business component test
Rhode Island corporate or personal income tax return (Form RI-1120C or applicable form)

name: South Carolina Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text:
The South Carolina Research and Development Tax Credit is authorized under S.C. Code Ann. § 12-6-3375 and administered by the South Carolina Department of Revenue (SCDOR). The credit is equal to 5% of a taxpayer's qualified research expenses (QREs) that exceed a base amount, mirroring the incremental structure of the federal research credit under IRC § 41. South Carolina conforms closely to the federal definition of qualified research under IRC § 41, meaning that expenses must satisfy the same four-part test: (1) the research must be conducted for a permitted purpose, (2) it must be technological in nature, (3) it must involve a process of experimentation, and (4) there must be an element of uncertainty. Activities qualifying for the federal credit generally also qualify for the South Carolina credit, provided they are conducted in connection with South Carolina business activities.

The base amount calculation generally follows the federal fixed-base percentage methodology. Taxpayers with insufficient gross receipts history to calculate a fixed-base percentage may use the start-up company rules analogous to IRC § 41. Qualifying expenses include wages paid to employees performing qualified research within South Carolina, supplies consumed in the conduct of qualified research in South Carolina, and contract research expenses for work performed in the state. Rental or lease costs of equipment used in qualifying research in South Carolina may also qualify.

The credit is non-refundable; it may only offset South Carolina income tax liability. Any unused credit may be carried forward for up to 10 years. There is no provision for transferability or sale of the credit to unrelated parties. The credit is available to both C corporations and pass-through entities (including S corporations, partnerships, and LLCs), with credit amounts flowing through to owners for their proportionate share.

Taxpayers must separately track and document South Carolina-based research activities distinct from any federal credit claim, since the SCDOR may audit the allocation of expenses between in-state and out-of-state research. It is important to note that while federal conformity provides a useful framework, credits should be calculated specifically on qualifying expenses attributable to South Carolina activities.

To claim the credit, taxpayers file Schedule TC-4 (Research and Development Credit) as part of their South Carolina income tax return (SC1120TC for corporations or SC1040TC for individuals and pass-through owners). The return, along with Schedule TC-4, is generally due on the same date as the state income tax return, including any extensions. See the South Carolina Department of Revenue at dor.sc.gov for current guidance, forms, and any rate updates.
documentation_required: Completed federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities)
South Carolina Schedule TC-4 (Research and Development Credit)
Payroll records identifying employees performing qualified research in South Carolina
Time-tracking or contemporaneous records allocating employee time to qualifying research activities
Receipts and invoices for research supplies consumed in South Carolina
Third-party contractor agreements and invoices for contract research performed in South Carolina
Project descriptions and technical narratives establishing the four-part IRC § 41 test
Base amount and fixed-base percentage calculations
Prior-year gross receipts records (for base period calculation)
Documentation of any carryforward credits from prior years

name: Utah Research Activities Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text:
The Utah Research Activities Credit is authorized under Utah Code §59-10-1012 (for individuals) and §59-7-612 (for corporations) and is administered by the Utah State Tax Commission. The credit is designed to encourage research and development activities conducted within Utah.

Credit Rate and Calculation: Utah's Research Activities Credit is calculated using a tiered approach that conforms broadly to the federal research credit framework under IRC §41. The primary credit rate is 5% of qualified research expenses (QREs) that exceed a base amount. Alternatively, for taxpayers who elect the simplified alternative, a rate of approximately 5% may apply to a simplified base calculation. Taxpayers should consult the Utah State Tax Commission or a qualified tax professional for the specific election methodology most beneficial to their situation, as rates and computational methods can vary. See the Utah State Tax Commission for the current applicable rates.

Qualifying Expenses: Qualified research expenses must relate to research activities conducted within the state of Utah. Utah generally conforms to the federal definition of qualified research expenses under IRC §41, which includes wages paid to employees engaged in qualified research, supplies used in qualified research, and a percentage of contract research expenses paid to third parties for research performed in Utah. Research must meet the four-part test: it must be technological in nature, intended to discover information, conducted for the purpose of developing a new or improved business component, and involve experimentation.

State-Specific Requirements: Unlike the federal credit, Utah requires that the qualified research activities be performed within the state of Utah. Expenses attributable to research conducted outside Utah are not eligible. This makes proper documentation of the location of research activities especially important for Utah filers.

Refundability and Transferability: The Utah Research Activities Credit is not refundable. Taxpayers cannot receive a cash refund if the credit exceeds their tax liability in a given year. The credit is also not transferable to other parties.

Carryforward: Unused credits may be carried forward for up to 14 years, allowing businesses that do not have sufficient tax liability in a given year to utilize the credit in future tax years. There is no carryback provision.

Filing: The credit is claimed on Utah Form TC-40R (Research Activities Credit) and is filed with the taxpayer's Utah corporate franchise and income tax return (Form TC-20) or individual income tax return (Form TC-40). Both pass-through entity members and corporate taxpayers may be eligible to claim the credit.

For authoritative guidance, current rates, and detailed instructions, taxpayers should consult the Utah State Tax Commission at tax.utah.gov.
documentation_required: Completed Utah Form TC-40R
Federal Form 6765 (Research Credit) or equivalent worksheets
Payroll records identifying employees engaged in qualified research activities in Utah
Time-tracking or allocation records for employees performing qualified research
Records of research supply expenses used in Utah-based research
Contracts and payment records for third-party contract research conducted in Utah
Project descriptions demonstrating the four-part test for qualified research
General ledger and accounting records supporting all QRE amounts claimed
Prior year QRE records for base period calculation
Documentation of business component being developed or improved

name: Vermont Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or personal income tax return
eligibility_text: The Vermont Research and Development Tax Credit is authorized under 32 V.S.A. § 5930a and administered by the Vermont Department of Taxes. For tax years beginning on or after January 1, 2016, Vermont allows a credit equal to 27% of the federal research and development tax credit computed under IRC § 41 that is attributable to Vermont activities. This means Vermont does not independently define qualified research expenses (QREs) but instead conforms closely to the federal framework under § 41, including the definition of qualified research, the distinction between basic research payments and in-house research expenses, and the computation of the base amount using either the regular credit method or the alternative simplified credit (ASC) method at the federal level. However, Vermont requires that the research activities be conducted within the state of Vermont in order to be included in the Vermont credit base. Only the portion of the federal § 41 credit attributable to Vermont-located research activities is eligible for the state credit. Taxpayers must therefore apportion or separately track which qualifying research expenses were incurred within Vermont versus other states. The 27% rate represents a meaningful enhancement to the federal benefit and is one of the more generous state R&D credit rates in New England. The Vermont R&D credit is not refundable; if the credit exceeds the taxpayer's Vermont tax liability in a given year, the unused portion may be carried forward for up to 10 tax years. There is no carryback provision. The credit is available to corporations subject to Vermont corporate income tax as well as pass-through entities (partnerships, S corporations, and sole proprietorships) whose owners are subject to Vermont personal income tax, with the credit flowing through to the individual owners. Taxpayers claiming the Vermont R&D credit must complete Form BA-404, which is the Technical Activity Tax Credit form used for several Vermont business tax credits. This form is filed with the applicable Vermont corporate income tax return (Form CO-411) or personal income tax return. The credit claimed on Form BA-404 must be supported by the federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities), and taxpayers should be prepared to document that the underlying research activities were physically conducted within Vermont. The Vermont Department of Taxes may request supporting documentation during examination, so contemporaneous records are strongly recommended. For the most current rate information, see the Vermont Department of Taxes at tax.vermont.gov.
documentation_required: Federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities)
Vermont Form BA-404 (Technical Activity Tax Credit)
Payroll records for Vermont-based research employees
Contracts and invoices for Vermont-based contract research
Project-level documentation identifying Vermont-located research activities
Supply receipts for research materials used in Vermont
Time-tracking or contemporaneous records allocating employee time to qualified research
Corporate income tax return (Form CO-411) or personal income tax return

name: Virginia Research and Development Expenses Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return, by the applicable due date (including extensions)
eligibility_text: Virginia offers the Research and Development Expenses Tax Credit under Va. Code § 58.1-339.4 (for individuals and pass-through entities) and § 58.1-432 (for corporations). The credit is available to taxpayers who incur qualified research and development expenses (QREs) in Virginia. For tax years beginning on or after January 1, 2011, the credit is equal to 15% of the first $300,000 in Virginia qualified research and development expenses, or 20% of the first $300,000 in Virginia QREs if the research was conducted in conjunction with a Virginia college or university. The maximum credit per taxpayer per year is $45,000 (15% of $300,000) or $60,000 (20% of $300,000 for university-partnered research). Virginia conforms to the federal definition of qualified research expenses under IRC § 41, meaning expenses must satisfy the four-part test: technological in nature, intended to discover information, conducted to eliminate uncertainty, and involve a process of experimentation. However, only expenses attributable to research activities conducted within Virginia are eligible — out-of-state research does not qualify. Virginia does not use a base amount or incremental calculation; the credit applies to a flat dollar cap of qualified expenses rather than an increase over a historical base, distinguishing it from the federal § 41 credit structure. The credit is not refundable; if the credit exceeds the taxpayer's tax liability for the year, the excess may be carried forward for up to 10 taxable years. The credit is not transferable. Taxpayers must apply for the credit by filing Form RDC with the Virginia Department of Taxation. Importantly, Virginia imposes an annual aggregate cap on this credit program: the total credits issued to all taxpayers in a given fiscal year is capped, and if applications exceed the cap, credits are allocated on a pro-rata basis among all qualifying applicants. The aggregate cap has historically been set at $7.76 million per year (see Virginia Department of Taxation for the current cap amount). Taxpayers should be aware that the pro-rata allocation may result in receiving less than the full computed credit if total applications exceed the cap in a given year. Businesses in any industry may qualify, provided the research activities are conducted in Virginia and meet the federal § 41 definition. For detailed guidance and current cap amounts, see the Virginia Department of Taxation at www.tax.virginia.gov.
documentation_required: Completed Virginia Form RDC (Research and Development Expenses Tax Credit application)
Federal Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities) or equivalent supporting schedules
Project-by-project description of qualified research activities conducted in Virginia
Payroll records identifying employees and wages allocable to Virginia research activities
Vendor invoices and contracts for supplies and contract research expenses
Records demonstrating research was performed within Virginia
Documentation of any university partnership (if claiming the 20% rate)
Virginia corporate income tax return (Form 500) or individual income tax return

name: West Virginia Research and Development Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with state corporate or personal income tax return
eligibility_text:
West Virginia offers a Research and Development Tax Credit under W. Va. Code §11-13Q-1 et seq. The credit is available to taxpayers engaged in qualified research activities within West Virginia. The headline credit rate is 10% of qualified research and development expenditures (QREs) incurred in West Virginia, with an additional 3% credit available for qualified expenditures made at research institutions or universities located in West Virginia, potentially yielding up to 13% in combined credits. QREs generally conform to the federal definition under IRC §41, covering wages paid to employees conducting qualified research, supplies used in research, and 65% of contract research expenses. The research activities must be performed within the state of West Virginia to qualify for the state credit; expenses attributable to activities outside WV are not eligible.

The credit may be applied against the taxpayer's West Virginia Business and Occupation Tax, Corporate Net Income Tax, or Personal Income Tax liability. The credit is non-refundable, meaning it cannot reduce a taxpayer's liability below zero or generate a cash refund. However, unused credit may be carried forward for up to 10 taxable years following the year in which the credit was earned, allowing businesses with limited current-year tax liability to still benefit from qualifying expenditures over time.

There is no specific company size or revenue threshold required to claim the West Virginia R&D credit; it is available to businesses of all sizes operating in the state. However, the credit is particularly beneficial for manufacturing, technology, energy, chemical, and life sciences companies that incur substantial qualifying research expenditures within the state. Start-up companies and small businesses with qualifying activities may also claim the credit, subject to their available tax liability.

To claim the credit, taxpayers must complete Schedule RDC and attach it to their West Virginia tax return. Taxpayers are encouraged to maintain contemporaneous records documenting the nature and purpose of research activities, payroll records, project logs, and vendor invoices to substantiate QRE claims. The West Virginia State Tax Department administers this credit; taxpayers should consult the Department's guidance and current regulations or contact the Department directly at tax.wv.gov for the most up-to-date information, as rates and rules may be subject to legislative change. See the West Virginia Department of Revenue (West Virginia State Tax Department) for current rate and eligibility details.
documentation_required: Completed West Virginia Schedule RDC
Federal Form 6765 (Research Credit)
Payroll records identifying employees and time spent on qualified research
Project descriptions and research activity logs
Vendor invoices and contracts for supply and contract research expenses
Documentation of research activities performed within West Virginia
Financial statements supporting QRE calculations
Base period expense records for prior years if applicable

name: Wisconsin Research Credits
filing_deadline: with state corporate or individual income tax return
eligibility_text:
Wisconsin offers a Research Credits program under Wis. Stat. § 71.28(4) (for corporations) and § 71.07(4) (for individuals and pass-through entities), administered by the Wisconsin Department of Revenue (DOR). The credit is available to businesses conducting qualified research activities in Wisconsin.

Credit Rates: The Wisconsin Research Credit is calculated at 5.75% of qualified research expenses (QREs) that exceed a base amount, closely conforming to the federal Internal Revenue Code § 41 methodology for determining the base period and incremental expenses. For certain taxpayers, an alternative simplified credit (ASC) method is also available, mirroring the federal ASC election. Wisconsin also provides an additional 5.75% credit for research conducted in conjunction with a qualified research consortium or educational institution, and a 7.5% credit for basic research payments to qualified educational institutions or scientific research organizations.

Qualified Research Expenses: Wisconsin generally conforms to the federal § 41 definition of QREs, including wages for qualified services, supplies used in research, and a portion of contract research expenses. Research activities must be conducted within Wisconsin to qualify for the state credit; expenses for research performed outside Wisconsin are not eligible.

Base Amount: Like the federal credit, Wisconsin uses a base period calculation tied to historical gross receipts and research expenses. Taxpayers with insufficient history may use the alternative simplified credit method.

Refundability and Transferability: The Wisconsin Research Credit is not refundable. Unused credits cannot be transferred or sold. However, for pass-through entities such as S corporations and partnerships, the credit may be passed through to individual owners and claimed on their Wisconsin individual income tax returns.

Carryforward: Unused Wisconsin Research Credits may be carried forward for up to 15 years, allowing businesses to utilize credits in future profitable tax years.

Additional Credits: Wisconsin also provides related credits for research expenses including a credit for research conducted using certain facilities and a credit tied to the Technology Zone Program for businesses located in designated technology zones.

Filing: The credit is claimed on Schedule R, which is attached to the Wisconsin corporate franchise/income tax return (Form 4 or Form 5) or the individual income tax return (Form 1). Pass-through entities report the credit on the entity-level return, and the credit flows to members or shareholders via Schedule 5K-1 or Schedule 3K-1.

For current rates, base period rules, and specific qualifying criteria, taxpayers should consult the Wisconsin Department of Revenue at https://www.revenue.wi.gov or review Wis. Stat. §§ 71.07(4) and 71.28(4).
documentation_required: Federal Form 6765 (or equivalent calculations)
Wisconsin Schedule R
Payroll records identifying wages paid to employees performing qualified research
Project-level descriptions of qualified research activities
Supply purchase records tied to qualified research
Contracts and invoices for contract research expenses
Base period gross receipts and QRE records (prior 4 years or as applicable)
Wisconsin source documentation demonstrating research was conducted in-state
Pass-through entity Schedule 5K-1 or 3K-1 if applicable
